words: LOUIS BOAS: Probably the best-he went back to Professor Baker's Drama
School at Princeton.
SAM SCHRAGER: This was Talbot Jennings?

speaker: LB
words: yes. You've heard ~ And of course later he went- he wrote several

speaker: LB
words: plays and they were produced in England, mostly. And then he joined

speaker: LB
words: the crew at Hollywood and became very familiar with Norma Shearer and

speaker: LB
words: her husband. And he wrote about three or four movies. Th^ *** Osca.r$.

speaker: SS
words: Mutiny on the Bounty, is one.

speaker: LB
words: Mutiny on the Bounty, with Charles Laughton and then Across the Wide

speaker: LB
words: Missouri. And Romeo and Juliet, he adapted for the movies. And The

speaker: LB
words: Good Earth and The Black Pearl, which did not succeed as well as the

speaker: LB
words: rest, but he had three or four. Northwest Passage, he wrote that and

speaker: LB
words: about four of them  . He's still living, West Glacier,

speaker: LB
words: Montana. We get a Christmas card from him.

speaker: SS
words: Who else was in that group that went out to write or make a living in

speaker: SS
words: English?

speaker: LB
words: Well, there was Blaine Stubblefield, who became editor of the notor

speaker: LB
words: and travel magazine, I can't tell you the name of it in New York and

speaker: LB
words: then he got tired of the rat race and came out to Weiser, Idaho and

speaker: LB
words: free-lanced and he was a great promoter of the Hell's Canyon as a recreation

speaker: LB
words: area. I believe he died^a few years ago. And Joel L. Priest

speaker: LB
words: who went to work for the Salt Lake Tribune, and then became the public

speaker: LB
words: relations man and writer for the Union Pacific Railroad. He's retired0"

speaker: LB
words: in Carmel, California. And my wife who did a little free-lancng

speaker: LB
words: and I entered the newspaper business after being a ghost writer for

speaker: LB
words: Westinghouse Electric at Pittsburgh. I spent a year with them and I

speaker: LB
words: couldn't stand Pittsburgh.

speaker: SS
words: Wasn't there one fellow who went on to Stanford?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, Buck, can't think of his first name now. Phillip Buck, who went

speaker: LB
words: on to Stanford and wrote some- he wrote work of higher class than we

speaker: LB
words: did. PhD type writing of papers and brochures on obscure subjects that

speaker: LB
words: English teachers write about. Analyzing or some par

speaker: LB
words: ticular abstract thought that the rest of us wouldn't see.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the English Department at the University at that

speaker: SS
words: time was strong?

speaker: LB
words: Very strong. Very strong. George Morey Miller was the head of the

speaker: LB
words: department and he was a martinet but he had a good department. And

speaker: LB
words: of course, John Cushman, who is still living, he's eighty-five, and

speaker: LB
words: he lives in Moscow for nine months of the year; he is the one who at

speaker: LB
words: tracted the people that were interested in writing. He had what he

speaker: LB
words: called a creative writing course and he attracted those peo pie to his

speaker: LB
words: course. And he encouraged Talbot Jennings to go on with his writing.

speaker: LB
words: He encouraged me to go on. And another of that era was Orin Fitzgerald

speaker: LB
words: who worked for the Salt Lake Tribune, and later came to Moscow as agri

speaker: LB
words: cultural editor. And he wrote literally hundreds of articles for agri

speaker: LB
words: cultural publications. He worked for a short time after he was here

speaker: LB
words: at the university, he left and went back to Boston as the editor of

speaker: LB
words: a publication for the Sheep Growers Association or something and he

speaker: LB
words: didn't like Boston, he was purely a Western man and gave it up and came

speaker: LB
words: back to Idaho. But he was a staff writer for several agricultural pub

speaker: LB
words: lications. One was- is it the American Farmer put out by the folks

speaker: LB
words: of the Saturday Evening Post?

speaker: SS
words: I'm not sure.

speaker: LB
words: I can't remember the name of it. It's still being published but he

speaker: LB
words: was a staff writer for them located in Moscow and he had an article

speaker: LB
words: about once a month. And he wrote for agricultural publications in

speaker: LB
words: Denver; one of them was a Cattle Association magazine at Denver that

speaker: LB
words: he wrote for. And the Idaho Farmer, he wrote for the Idaho Farmer

speaker: LB
words: published in Spokane. He did a lot of writing.

speaker: SS
words: You say that George Morey Miller was sort of a martinet, what do you

speaker: SS
words: mean by that? He ran the department—

speaker: LB
words: He ran the department with an iron hand and if you made a mistake in

speaker: LB
words: English he just climbed all over you. In other words, he wouldn't al

speaker: LB
words: low you to split an infinitive and he wouldn't allow you to say, "the

speaker: LB
words: team, they", and you read the modern newspapers, that's^what they do.

speaker: LB
words: The city council, they did so-and-so. And my wife says that in later

speaker: LB
words: years that became acceptable. Well it never did to me! When I was

speaker: LB
words: editor of the Idahonian if I saw the story that"the team they did soand-

speaker: LB
words: so,"l corrected it, and told the reporterKthat was verboten. And

speaker: LB
words: my wife said, "Oh, they accept that now." I suppose the English lan

speaker: LB
words: guage grows by usage.

speaker: SS
words: In other words, team would be singular rather than plural?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, it is singular. What would you say with two teams?

speaker: SS
words: That's plural.

speaker: LB
words: Okay, what do you say with one team when you say "they"? (Chuckles)

speaker: LB
words: Get my point? I think it should be singular at all times. And I no

speaker: LB
words: tice in the newspapers they talk of so-and-so that was elected city

speaker: LB
words: councilman. Well, of course, it should be who. And then they will

speaker: LB
words: say, "The horses who ran over the grade and so forth," it should be

speaker: LB
words: what or that. They confuse the personal pronoun, either leaving it

speaker: LB
words: out and using another reference word or use a personal pronoun when

speaker: LB
words: it should be the impersonal pronoun. This is done all the time. I

speaker: LB
words: seei the Lewiston Tribune, and I see it in all publications, exBOAS

speaker: LB
words: cept I don't see it in the Wall Street Journal, which is one publica

speaker: LB
words: tion that is factually correct. I never noticed that in the Journal.

speaker: SS
words: You know, speaking about Miller; did he strike you as he seemed to

speaker: SS
words: strike a lot of people in Moscow as being a character?

speaker: LB
words: He was a character. He was eccentric. No question about it! He was

speaker: LB
words: an eccentric character, although his majors adored him. Say that he

speaker: LB
words: was a fine man. And he insisted upon correct usage of the English

speaker: LB
words: language, there is no question about it. And they used to call him

speaker: LB
words: an eccentric because you had to buy a tablet of paper to use for your

speaker: LB
words: themes which carried on the cover his rules for the usage of English.

speaker: LB
words: And he made a royalty on selling those. The freshmen had to buy that

speaker: LB
words: booklet. And he was criticized for that, which is wrong, no question

speaker: LB
words: about it. (Chuckles) I don't think a professor should have a racket

speaker: LB
words: of that nature, where he makes you buy a certain publication because

speaker: LB
words: he's got a royalty on it. George Morey did. But he required and in

speaker: LB
words: stilled in the freshmen- at that time we had two general lectures a

speaker: LB
words: week from George Morey Miller on English.

speaker: SS
words: All freshmen?

speaker: LB
words: All of them in one group. It was either two or one. They all met in

speaker: LB
words: the auditorium and he lectured the entire freshman class in English.

speaker: SS
words: On usage, primarily?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, usage and literature, whatever happened to be his . We had

speaker: LB
words: a general meeting of all freshmen English students.

speaker: SS
words: Was it good?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, it was good. They way they do, and I think they still do in

speaker: LB
words: chemistry I, have a general lecture by department man once a week and

speaker: LB
words: then you go to your individual classes for additional lectures and

speaker: LB
words: questions and answers and quizzes.

speaker: SS
words: Let me get this straight; this was for the whole- just the English

speaker: SS
words: students, or the whole class?

speaker: LB
words: Freshmen. The whole class.

speaker: SS
words: No matter what your major was?

speaker: LB
words: No, no matter what your major was. And of course, Freshmen English

speaker: LB
words: was required. The entire freshmen class met once or twice a week, I

speaker: LB
words: can't remember which, in the auditorium for George Morey Miller's lec

speaker: LB
words: ture on English. And then you spent, say, two periods a week with

speaker: LB
words: your individual instructor in which you wrote themes, answered oral

speaker: LB
words: quizzes and took your written quizzes.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think at that time the presence of men like Miller and Cushman

speaker: SS
words: had an effect on attracting students to English as a major?

speaker: LB
words: John Cushman did. He attracted people who were interested in writing;

speaker: LB
words: definitely. I don't know that George Morey Miller did; no. But we had

speaker: LB
words: a man here who was an outstanding authority on history; Hulme, who went

speaker: LB
words: from here to Stanford. And he was one of the, at least United States

speaker: LB
words: authorities and I think a world authority on European History. And

speaker: LB
words: he was here in the early '20's and left about 1921. He was a fine man.

speaker: LB
words: And he attracted students here for history. And he became- he was re

speaker: LB
words: cognized while he was here and after he left)he was recognized as a

speaker: LB
words: worldwide authority on history. The University of Idaho now has two

speaker: LB
words: worldwide authorities in science; Dr.Peck in physics, he's a world au

speaker: LB
words: thority on certain phases of physics. Had a friend of mine who's son

speaker: LB
words: graduated in physics from Stanford University and came here for his

speaker: LB
words: because he couldn't afford to go on to Stanford any more and

speaker: LB
words: he was amazed to find that Dr. Peck was here as a member of the physics

speaker: LB
words: department when he had studied his book at Palo Alto- Stanford. Other

speaker: LB
words: of course, worldwide authority is Miss Jeannine- she married Gretver.

speaker: LB
words: She's a worldwide authority today on fluorocarbon chemistry. Do you

speaker: LB
words: remember her?

speaker: SS
words: No, I don't know her.

speaker: LB
words: You don't know her?

speaker: SS
words: No.

speaker: LB
words: Jeannine Shreave. And she's traveled all over the world giving lec

speaker: LB
words: tures on fluorocarbon. It's an accident that we got her, she came

speaker: LB
words: from Montana as an unknown, as a young graduate, a young PhD, and she

speaker: LB
words: was interested in fluorocarbons and she had her candidates for a master's

speaker: LB
words: degrees and Phd's^on fluorocarbons and she became a world authority

speaker: LB
words: I think we have a fewer percentage of those now than we had in 1920,

speaker: LB
words: because we had some very good teachers. N A. W. Fahrenwald in mining,

speaker: LB
words: who is still living, and he's a world authority on mining problems.

speaker: LB
words: He developed flotation processes for separating minerals from ores.

speaker: LB
words: And he's been called all over the world to consult mining companies.

speaker: LB
words: He went to Cypress a number of years ago. Well, he's been retired for

speaker: LB
words: twenty years. And we had a better faculty overall than we have today.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think the difference has been in time?

speaker: LB
words: I don't know. I do not know. Except that it was a small school, we

speaker: LB
words: had a small community; the people liked the climate. I think we were

speaker: LB
words: paying a comparable wage at that time, which is nothing what it is now.

speaker: LB
words: They got $7,000 or $8,000 a year and now they get $25,000 a year and

speaker: LB
words: yell. Except in English, they're in demand more than they were then.

speaker: LB
words: We've had an explosion in the number of students all over the world.

speaker: LB
words: It's gotten to the point that if you don't go to college you don't amount

speaker: LB
words: to anything; that's wrong.

speaker: SS
words: I wondered if the competition among the three Idaho schools would have

speaker: SS
words: an effect as well because of the demands from BSU and ISU for funds.

speaker: LB
words: I'm sure that three institutions do mean that we have to spread it

speaker: LB
words: thinner. We have to spread our support for higher education between

speaker: LB
words: three institutions rather than one. And that's the way it was; it was

speaker: LB
words: one then. know/Idaho State College was known then as Idaho

speaker: LB
words: Technical Institute. It was purely a technical institute where you

speaker: LB
words: learned barbering and cosmetology and auto mechanics and so forth.

speaker: LB
words: Then it became a branch of the University; the Southern Branch then

speaker: LB
words: it became an independent institution; two year institution then a

speaker: LB
words: four year institution and now it's giving graduate work and they

speaker: LB
words: want to give Phds. You can't, you can't for 750,000 people, you can't

speaker: LB
words: afford that. It's like putting three degree gramHng institutions

speaker: LB
words: supported by the public money of the the city of Seattle. Can't be

speaker: LB
words: done, in my estimation.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that there was concern at the University of Idaho back

speaker: SS
words: in the '20's about it's development?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, certainly. It's always been here as long as I've been around Moscow.

speaker: LB
words: And we though we had politically,^ people

speaker: LB
words: thought they'd solved the problem when they made Pocatello , the In

speaker: LB
words: stitution of Pocatello, was asking for college status instead of an

speaker: LB
words: institute, and it was accepted by the legislature as a branch of the

speaker: LB
words: University and it became a two year college. So we thought we'd sol

speaker: LB
words: ved that, that they were under us. We controlled it and we sent a

speaker: LB
words: dean from Moscow to Pocatello to operate it.

speaker: SS
words: That was Dean-

speaker: LB
words: Angell. Dean Angell, who's daughter lives two doors from here.

speaker: SS
words: Dusault.

speaker: LB
words: Mrs. Dusalt. We thought we'd solved it, because it was part of us;

speaker: LB
words: the Southern Branch, we could hold it. You couldn't hold it.

speaker: SS
words: What happened?

speaker: LB
words: They got the bigger population and they got the people down there so

speaker: LB
words: they voted to make it an independent institution; maJ(e it a four year

speaker: LB
words: school. And of course, Boise started out as a junior college, with

speaker: LB
words: a junior college district which encompassed all of Ada County as a

speaker: LB
words: taxing unit, you see. And I think it took in some of the smaller

speaker: LB
words: areas around Ada County, probably part of Payette County and so forth

speaker: LB
words: as a taxing unit. And they supported that as a junior college. Well,

speaker: LB
words: you know, you get 75,000 people in an area, there's another 75,000 in

speaker: LB
words: the environs and Nampa and Caldwell and Payette and so forth, why, they

speaker: LB
words: wanted their own school, so they made it a four year school. And mark

speaker: LB
words: my words, I won't live to see it, you will, when Boise is the major

speaker: LB
words: institution in the state of Idaho. No question about it. Just ten

speaker: LB
words: days ago the State Board of Education approved a course, a fo ur year

speaker: LB
words: course, in legal technology to graduate people with enough legal know

speaker: LB
words: ledge that they could do the paper work ot the lawyers and let the

speaker: LB
words: lawyers free- did you read about that?

speaker: SS
words: No, but I know the course you're talking about because I know-

speaker: LB
words: Just in the paper ten days ago. That's the beginning of law school.

speaker: LB
words: You wait, four years from now, they'll say, "We've got this, why not

speaker: LB
words: have the law school to follow it?" It's coming.

speaker: SS
words: How much do you think that the university here is protected by it's

speaker: SS
words: alumni who have gone on to become the leaders?

speaker: LB
words: Very little. Who are the leaders for advancing Boise College to graduate

speaker: LB
words: status and PhD status, and the law school? Who are they? They're the

speaker: LB
words: lawyers of Boise who all graduated from the University of Idaho. They

speaker: LB
words: tried to move the law school down to Boise two years ago. They tried

speaker: LB
words: four years ago to move it; who were the prime movers? The lawyers of

speaker: LB
words: Boise, ninety percent of who graduated right out here. So you can t

speaker: LB
words: depend upon that, in my estimation.

speaker: SS
words: What does protect the university then?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, the fact that it's been here; it's the oldest school, it has pres

speaker: LB
words: tige and it has age, but that won't protect it forever. Boise's got

speaker: LB
words: the population. Boise itself is 100,000 people. Spokane's 180,000;

speaker: LB
words: well, Boise's getting up there.

speaker: SS
words: So you think ultimately it's a question -

speaker: LB
words: Power politics. Power politics. Boise's population ^ them as

speaker: LB
words: many representatives in the Senate and House as all of Idaho.

speaker: LB
words: And they're going to get what they want; it's bound to be. Just bound

speaker: LB
words: to be. That's why Frank Church didn't get to be vice president, he

speaker: LB
words: comes from a little state; no influence. That's why Sweicher was cho

speaker: LB
words: sen by Regan because he could influence 110 delegates in Pennsylvania,

speaker: LB
words: or so he thought Power politics.

speaker: SS
words: It makes me think of the old story about the origination of the univer

speaker: SS
words: sity here rather than down there. I'm just curious about the version

speaker: SS
words: of that that you've heard. Did you hear it as a way of keeping North

speaker: SS
words: Idaho in the state?

speaker: LB
words: Yes. Definitely. Idaho was on the verge of seceding and joining with

speaker: LB
words: -to western Montana and eastern Washington ^the state of Lincoln. In

speaker: LB
words: fact, there was a bill passed by the legislature to create that state

speaker: LB
words: and it was vetoed by President Cleveland; I believe it was President

speaker: LB
words: Cleveland. It was vetoed; it passed. And there is no question about

speaker: LB
words: it, they demanded something up here and Moscow was one of the leading

speaker: LB
words: communities of North Idaho. Moscow and Lewiston and they put it in

speaker: LB
words: Moscow. No question. They had a couple of smart men down there as

speaker: LB
words: representatives and so forth. One was Forney, Judge Forney. He was

speaker: LB
words: there and he was a smart man and he persuaded them to give the university of Idaho.

speaker: LB
words: And they thought that was of minor importance and

speaker: LB
words: that'll mean fifty or seventy-five people going to the university

speaker: LB
words: and so forth and so on. No question about that.

speaker: SS
words: You mean, that that's what they thought? They didn't realize the im

speaker: SS
words: plication?

speaker: LB
words: No, they did not. Because state universities were not too popular.

speaker: LB
words: There was one in California and I suppose one in other states; Washington had one.

speaker: LB
words: .But it wasn't considered any great big plum.

speaker: SS
words: You yourself were raised in Boise, weren't you?

speaker: LB
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: How do you feel than? Do you feel divided loyalties at all?

speaker: LB
words: No. Because I haven't been back to Boise since I was about twenty

speaker: LB
words: years old. Oh, I've been back for visits. We came out from Ohio in

speaker: LB
words: 1910 and when I was eighteen I came to the University of Idaho and

speaker: LB
words: stayed here for a couple of years and the recession came along of

speaker: LB
words: 1920 and '21, and I knew my dad was a little hard pressed so I went

speaker: LB
words: to work and earned my way through school from then on. And was only

speaker: LB
words: back there one year, I went to Westinghouse at Pittsburgh as a ghost

speaker: LB
words: writer. I graduated as an engineer. And I knew when I graduated that

speaker: LB
words: was not an engineer, but I had the degree. In other words, I didn't

speaker: LB
words: like engineering. I liked chemistry and always have, but I didn't like

speaker: LB
words: the engineering that went with it. Mathmatics was a headache. So,

speaker: LB
words: the fact that Westinghouse needed a writer and I could understand the

speaker: LB
words: language I got a job. And I wrote articles for technical and semi-tech

speaker: LB
words: nical publications which carried the name of the scientist or engineer

speaker: LB
words: that did the work. I wrote it; he did the work; he couldn't write; I

speaker: LB
words: couldn't do the work. You get my point? And the term had not been de

speaker: LB
words: vised at that time. I never heard the term ghostwriter until much later.

speaker: LB
words: That's what I was, I was a technical writer. And I stayed in Pittsburgh

speaker: LB
words: for one year.

speaker: SS
words: Why did you leave it?

speaker: LB
words: Leave? Well, if you had seen Pittsburgh in those days you would have

speaker: LB
words: known why. They've cleaned it up since then, but it was the dirtiest

speaker: LB
words: place in the world. And the plant where I worked was right out in the

speaker: LB
words: east end, Turtle Creek or what they called East . Pittsburgh and

speaker: LB
words: populated with ethnic minorities, which were commonly referred to as

speaker: LB
words: "hunkies", or Negroes. We lived in another area six miles away; Wilkinsburg,

speaker: LB
words: which is a nice little town but it was a dirty town. And I

speaker: LB
words: didn't have any friends there and I had a girl out here and that was

speaker: LB
words: that! So, I quit.

speaker: SS
words: Did you feel that the West was the place to be rather than the East?

speaker: LB
words: Then I did, yes. When I graduated from college I thought you had to

speaker: LB
words: go East to be anybody. If you wanted to amount to something you had

speaker: LB
words: to get back East, so I went back East. And when I got there I was

speaker: LB
words: disillusioned.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that was a common attitude among the college graduate^?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, I think it was. I think it was. It was the place where the big

speaker: LB
words: money was. It was the place where life was interesting. It was a

speaker: LB
words: place where people advanced. There wasn't anything out here, at least

speaker: LB
words: in Idaho. You didn't have any industries in the West. Now California's

speaker: LB
words: full of them. But we didn't have those.

speaker: SS
words: The attraction would be East rather than Seattle, Portland or Cali

speaker: SS
words: fornia at that time?

speaker: LB
words: Yes. Oh, yes. People came to California to retire. There was no in

speaker: LB
words: dustry of any major import. I wanted to go East; be in the big time

speaker: LB
words: and so forth and so on. And one year there and the dirt and the filthit

speaker: LB
words: was just awful dirty, they had not cleaned it up. Air pollution

speaker: LB
words: was terrible! Smoke- you couldn't sit on the grass, ever! You had

speaker: LB
words: to put a newspaper down when you sit on the grass. It was just soot.

speaker: LB
words: And it was just awful. And I took it for one year.

speaker: SS
words: And it sounds as though the cities were socially disorganized, too.

speaker: LB
words: Well, I didn't know. I was young and I didn't know anybody. I had

speaker: LB
words: a roommate who was from up at Weippe. One of the Space boys, Ralph

speaker: LB
words: Space. He since died. There were three or four of 'em, and all of

speaker: LB
words: 'em brilliant. And he was my roommate and he left before I did. He

speaker: LB
words: stuck it out about eight or nine months and then he had to get out

speaker: LB
words: here in the wild area of the Weippe region and became a forester. And

speaker: LB
words: he was an electrical engineer. And I stayed a little longer and fi

speaker: LB
words: nally just gave it up.

speaker: SS
words: Let me go back to the decision of going to college; talk a little bit

speaker: SS
words: about the college years here. I'm curious about the way that you

speaker: SS
words: would be going to college. Was it a foregone conclusion?

speaker: LB
words: No, it wasn't. Minority of students at that time went to college. I

speaker: LB
words: don't know what the percentage was; fifteen percent; twenty. It was

speaker: LB
words: minority, but I wanted to go to college and my dad wanted to

speaker: LB
words: go to college and I was interested in chemistry; that was my favorite

speaker: LB
words: topic in high school, so I came up here to be a chemist. And they told

speaker: LB
words: me the best route was chemical engineering, so I took chemical engineering

speaker: SS
words: Had either of your parents gone to college?

speaker: LB
words: No, neither of them. My dad was a candy maker. He had a store in

speaker: LB
words: Boise.

speaker: SS
words: There was a value that he placed on going to school?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, yes, there was a value he placed on education, that's right. And

speaker: LB
words: my friends came to college; almost all of 'em. Those that I ran around

speaker: LB
words: with. They almost all came to college.

speaker: SS
words: To Moscow?

speaker: LB
words: Yes. Those of my group came to college. Ted Sherman who lives in

speaker: LB
words: Moscow is one of them. And that's about the size of the ones that are

speaker: LB
words: here. We came to college, and we all finished.

words: sity of Michigan, I forget which for one or two years. I don't know

words: if he flunked out. I don't know if he ran out of money or what, but

words: train, she was chaparone. At a certain time of night, why, if you

speaker: LB
words: Student Special!

speaker: LB

words: of Women at Idaho. She died about twelve, fifteen years ago, way up

words: nasium, which is now the women's gym. And they had an all college

words: beer to begin with, of course, we still had Prohibition. But she ran

words: they bailed her out, and they bought it not as much as she paid for

speaker: LB
words: it, but they bailed her out and she got most of her money back and then

speaker: SS
words: bail her out.

words: Student Union and the main stairway going up to a landing and then

words: coming back this way to go up to the main big hall of the Student

words: was gone, and I was curious, didn't see it. Oh, not many weeks later

words: stopped him and I said, "Jim, I noticed a week or two ago that the

speaker: LB
words: portrait of Dean French was no longer hanging in the Student Union

speaker: LB
words: building. "Oh," he said, "I hadn't noticed it." I said, "Well, it's

words: not there. I don't know where it is, maybe it's been moved." I says,

speaker: LB
words: I'm telling you now that I'm cutting my pleqge in two for the perfor

speaker: LB
words: ming arts building unless I get a satisfactory explanation of why

speaker: LB

words: been $2,500, I don't know, but at least $1,500. It was done by an ar

words: tist in Seattle where Dean French was then living. And it was juat a

speaker: LB
words: quiet movement to raise the money, and we raised the money, I maybe

words: gave $10 and a lot of others did, and she cane over to see it, and we

words: he said, "I understand you're a little disturbed about something Louif,

speaker: LB
words: would you come up and talk to me? And I said, "Be glad to." So I

speaker: LB
words: says, "I feel that way very strongly." He said, "Well, we have de

words: cided to put the photographs of all the deans in one room at the liball

speaker: LB
words: rary, and line the walls the pictures ofAthe deans, past

speaker: LB
words: and present. And so, that will go there." And I said, "Yes, and

words: "This one of Dean French an expensive, at that

speaker: LB
words: time, oil painting." I said, "It's fine to have the deans' photo

words: Blue Bucket Inn, and went broke at it. She did it as a service to the

words: belongs in the Student^Union." I said, "I believe in tradition." I

words: the same names; so-and-so's hall. It's true in the University of Lon

speaker: LB

speaker: SS
words: She sounds like an extremely dedicated person.

speaker: LB
words: She was. She had various rules, in other words, no girl could appear

speaker: LB
words: on campus in anything but a skirt, except when the temperature got down

speaker: LB
words: below a certain degree in winter. Ten above zero or zero, or something.

speaker: LB
words: And then they could wear slacks. Otherwise, they wore skirts. No

speaker: LB
words: question about it. You were home by nine o'clock week nights, you

speaker: LB
words: were in your dormitory or sorority. Period! Saturday night and Fri

speaker: LB
words: day nights you were in by eleven-thirty« and Sunday night by seven.

speaker: LB
words: And it was a rule, no one questioned it. She was very strict. But

speaker: LB
words: the students as such disliked her and as graduates they loved her.

speaker: LB
words: That's correct. I thought she was the weeds! (Chuckles) I didn't like

speaker: LB
words: her, and yet she was always invited to every fraternity and every so

speaker: LB
words: rority dance. Not to invite Dean French was unthinkable.

speaker: SS
words: While undergraduates?

speaker: LB
words: While undergraduates, oh, yes, it was unthinkable. And some student

speaker: LB
words: would be assigned to escort her and they did. And everybody in the

speaker: LB
words: house, every man in the house danced with her, always. You danced with

speaker: LB
words: her, maybe you had to cut in, but you danced with her and " apprecia

speaker: LB
words: ted it that it was a courtesy. She did this; she would announce to

speaker: LB
words: a fraternity and a sorority that, "I would like to be invited to din

speaker: LB
words: ner next Sunday. I want to give a little ten minute talk after dinner."

speaker: LB
words: And so forth, so you invited her. And her talk at the dinner table

speaker: LB
words: was on table manners and common courtesy. And you didn't see students

speaker: LB
words: after that shoveling food in their mouths with the wrong hand or hol

speaker: LB
words: ding their fork this way. She did that every fall. And she taught

speaker: LB
words: them manners. And it was part of the college career to learn to be

speaker: LB
words: ladies and gentlemen. No one does that now.

words: would come to school?

speaker: LB

speaker: SS

words: vise the morals of the campus and be dean of women and see that the wo

speaker: LB
words: the boys were gone by a certain time of night, and that was that. And

speaker: LB
words: including myself-

speaker: LB

speaker: LB
words: this boy's. That was opinion of the girls, of the ones that would allow

words: Out of state, yes. To a better school. In other words, they, oh,

words: tracks. Heavens yesI You didn't have a car. There was no picture

words: Well, in those days. This just something that strikes me from reading;

speaker: SS
words: then as compared to now. Do you think- like today, men are never expacted

speaker: SS
words: to get involved in or things like that. That's a thing

speaker: SS
words: of the past as far as experience in growing up. But do you think on

speaker: SS
words: the campus in those days, that was more accepted for what men would

speaker: SS
words: do to get experience?

speaker: LB
words: It was no more accepted, but I think that more men did it, yes. They

speaker: LB
words: used to hike off to Lewiston. Lewiston was lumbermen's and mining

speaker: LB
words: town. The miners and lumbermen would come into Lewiston once every

speaker: LB
words: six weeks to blow their wages.

speaker: SS
words: Blowing in?

speaker: LB
words: Yes. There were a certain number of houses and certain students would

speaker: LB
words: see to that themselves. They could take care of it. They'd go down

speaker: LB
words: to Lewiston on the train or otherwise. Yes. Not a great percentage

speaker: LB
words: of 'em, but a few. But if they propositioned their own girl, why,

speaker: LB
words: they'd get slapped in the face and that was that!

speaker: SS
words: And lose her?

speaker: LB
words: That's right, they'd lose her.

speaker: SS
words: You know, there's a lot of discussion of women's liberation these daysand

speaker: SS
words: I'm wondering about how much equality between the sexes there was

speaker: SS
words: in those days? I know, for instance, that women didn't seem to have

speaker: SS
words: the opportunities to get involved in a lot of different fields of study

speaker: SS
words: that they do today. Their career opportunities seem to have been re

speaker: SS
words: latively restricted.

speaker: LB
words: That's right.. There were no women in the college of engineering.

speaker: LB
words: And if a girl had enrolled in the college of engineering the dean

speaker: LB
words: would have done everything to discourage her. It was a man's field.

speaker: LB
words: I remember Bernice Day, who had this big house on the corner here, the

speaker: LB
words: Day home, and he and his brothers owned the Day Mine in the Coeur d'

speaker: LB
words: Alenes and had a big stock in the Bunker Hill and Sullivan Mines.

speaker: LB
words: Jerome Day was one of the discoverers of the Bunker Hill and Sullivan

speaker: LB
words: Mine, and he had a daughter; he had a son and a daughter and his son

speaker: LB
words: was drowned on Lake Washington in Seattle as a boy of about nineteen.

speaker: LB
words: And it broke his father's heart. And he had a daughter and she was

speaker: LB
words: huge in size, and she took mining engineering. And that was just the

speaker: LB
words: scandal of the campus. But she did it and she graduated. But it was

speaker: LB
words: almost unheard of. She was a freak to take mining engineering. She

speaker: LB
words: died a number of years ago. She was younger than I am, but she died

speaker: LB
words: because she was huge in size and. was.a mortal cinch she wouldn't

speaker: LB
words: live to old age.

speaker: SS
words: Why do you think the attitudes were as they were at that time? About

speaker: SS
words: that sort of thing? It doesn't seem sinful to me for a woman who wants

speaker: SS
words: to study mining engineering to take it. But that's my_ generation.

speaker: SS
words: LB* Well, the woman's place was in the home. She should take home economics

speaker: SS
words: or English or languages or some of the fine arts courses. She wasn't

speaker: SS
words: supposed to earn a living. The man earned the living. She was sup

speaker: SS
words: posed to stay home and raise the family, so why take engineering? She

speaker: SS
words: couldn't get a job. Of course now it's different, you see you've got

speaker: SS
words: equality, but in those days you couldn't get a job if you were a woman

speaker: SS
words: engineer! Hopeless. You couldn't practice law because you'd be ostra

speaker: SS
words: cised. There were no women lawyers in those days. Just the same as a

speaker: SS
words: boy would never enroll in home economics. A few of 'em do now! Just

speaker: SS
words: the way the world was built. It's different now! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Do you feel that on an intellectual plane that women were regarded as

speaker: SS
words: less competent?

speaker: LB
words: In certain fields, yes. It bothered the hell out of me when Dean

speaker: LB
words: Angell's daughter was enrolled in my calculus course as a sophmore.

speaker: LB
words: I was a sophomore in engineering. She majored in mathematics, and she

speaker: LB
words: was in my calculus course. The only woman I ever had in any course

speaker: LB
words: related to engineering. And she's a good friend of mine. Lives two

speaker: LB
words: doors down here; Mary Evelyn Angell Dusault. But she took calculus

speaker: LB
words: in my course, and the men resented it. I resented it. Probably because

speaker: LB
words: she was smarter than most of the men! .. But they just didn't like

speaker: LB
words: it! They didn't like it! I can tell you a story if you're interested

speaker: LB
words: about the teacher of that course. Taylor, Professor Eugene Taylor.

speaker: LB
words: And I think it was in the calculus course that he announced at the be

speaker: LB
words: ginning of the year that he had been teaching for seventeen years; he

speaker: LB
words: had never missed a class in his life; he had never been late for classes

speaker: LB
words: in his life and he said that if any time the bell rings and I'm not

speaker: LB
words: here at my desk, it means that something unusual has happened and

speaker: LB
words: that's that! You may be free to go. On the other hand, if one of you

speaker: LB
words: students is not in his chair when the bell rings, you're not present!

speaker: LB
words: You can stay, but you're not present. And that was that. And throug

speaker: LB
words: hout the course, he'd be lecturing, or putting something on the black

speaker: LB
words: board and the bell would ring and he'd lay his chalk down. That's

speaker: LB
words: the end of the course, you never kept you five secondsovertime. One

speaker: LB
words: day the bell rang and Professor Taylor was not in the room. So we all

speaker: LB
words: get up and walked out. Opened the door and there was Professor Taylor

speaker: LB
words: just coming up. "Morning, Mr. Taylor." "Good morning gentlemen".

speaker: LB
words: kept on going. And the next morning he said, "Gentlemen, I made

speaker: LB
words: a mistake," he said, "I've been in the habit of accepting a ride from

speaker: LB
words: one of my neighbors, Dean Messenger of the college of engineering.

speaker: LB
words: Yesterday we had a flat tire. I was late. Gentleman, from now on I

speaker: LB
words: shall walk." And he walked to class. He lived over here, just as

speaker: LB
words: far from the campus as I do; he walked to class every day until he re

speaker: LB
words: tired about forty years later. He never rode. I used to go to work

speaker: LB
words: in the morning at seven-thirty and I'd pass him going down Third Street.

speaker: LB
words: He walked the rest of his life. He only died about two years ago, up

speaker: LB
words: in his hineties. But about five or six years ago I was at a cocktail party of a friend

speaker: LB
words: who lived right near there and the Taylors were invited. And I saw

speaker: LB
words: Mrs. Taylor there and I said, "You know, I got a good story to tell

speaker: LB
words: about your husband." And I started to tell her and she says, "My God

speaker: LB
words: were you in that class?" And I said, "Yes." She said, "We have tal

speaker: LB
words: ked about that many and many a time. Go over and tell Gene that you

speaker: LB
words: were in that class. .1 did, and he enjoyed it. But everyone knows

speaker: LB
words: that Gene- that Eugene Taylor walked to and from class every day for

speaker: LB
words: forty years, and that's the reason.

speaker: SS
words: This passion for punctuality; was that the way he taught, as well?

speaker: LB
words: That's the way he taught. Yes sir! He flunked me in an exam one time.

speaker: LB
words: I think it was calculus, it might have been engineering mathematics,

speaker: LB
words: I don't know, and he asked me to stay after class, and he gave me a

speaker: LB
words: flunk in, oh, a periodic exam, it wasn't a final exam and he said,

speaker: LB
words: "Mr. Boas," he said, "I flunked you on that course. You did the pro

speaker: LB
words: blem right, but you made a mistake in arithmetic." And he said, "You

speaker: LB
words: know, you are poor enough student, you can't afford that!" From that

speaker: LB
words: day to this I brag, I never make a mistake in arithmetic. I do, occassionally,

speaker: LB
words: but I'm particular, I see that the arithmetic is correct.

speaker: LB
words: Because I did the problem right, but I got the wrong answer. Didn't

speaker: LB
words: give me anything; he gave me zero. Some professors would have given

speaker: LB
words: you half credit for doing the problem right, but I had made a mistake

speaker: LB
words: in arithmetic. He lived mathematics. His life was mathematics from

speaker: LB
words: the time he got up til the time he went to bed. He was prompt; he

speaker: LB
words: lived mathematics and he was my favorite professor. He and Dr. Costellic

speaker: LB
words: in chemistry are my two favorite professors.They were different,

speaker: LB
words: entirely different.

speaker: SS
words: What is it about them that--?

speaker: LB
words: Well, they were just good .. They were just excellent teachers.

speaker: LB
words: Everyone thought that Taylor was an excellent teacher, and he was an

speaker: LB
words: excellent teacher.

speaker: SS
words: By excellent, do you mean ?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, oh, yes. He'd work with you; he'd answer questions. You could

speaker: LB
words: go to his office anytime and he would take time out to explain things.

speaker: LB
words: And he was never too busy to help you, but he lived by the rules. If

speaker: LB
words: you weren't in your seat when the bell rang, you were not present!

speaker: LB
words: And, that's that!

speaker: SS
words: Does that mean you couldn't ask any questions?

speaker: LB
words: You couldn't ask any questions, you couldn't turn a paper in, you

speaker: LB
words: could not participate in the discussion. You could listen, but you

speaker: LB
words: couldn't do anything else; you were not present. And believe me, every

speaker: LB
words: body was in his seat when the course started!

speaker: SS
words: When you first came to Moscow, what did you think of the town, as a

speaker: SS
words: freshman student here?

speaker: LB
words: It was a lousy town. We all made fun of it. It was small; didn't have

speaker: LB
words: any good stores; the movie theatre was a small theatre on Third Street.

speaker: LB
words: And before I graduated they had the one down on Main; just one of them.

speaker: LB
words: But it was a poor town, no one liked it, they all made fun of little

speaker: LB
words: Moscow. But of course, actually, it was a nice little town. It's the

speaker: LB
words: way you believe, you know. Home is best; home is good; home is fine,

speaker: LB
words: this is no place to be.

speaker: SS
words: Well, it certainly wasn't very urban compared to Boise at the time, I

speaker: SS
words: imagine.

speaker: LB
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Did you become involved in fraternities very quickly after you came

speaker: SS
words: here?

speaker: LB
words: The second year; Sigma Chi.

speaker: SS
words: What made you decide on that one?

speaker: LB
words: Well, that was new, I was one of the organizers of it. There were

speaker: LB
words: only about four fraternities at that time, and there was room for more.

speaker: LB
words: And during that period there were three or four fraternities started.

speaker: LB
words: Sigma Chi was started, Alpha Tau Omega was started, the Pi Phis were

speaker: LB
words: started and TKEs later. There were four or five started. At that

speaker: LB
words: time there were only about four. Kappa Sig, Beta, Theta - or Phi Del

speaker: LB
words: ta Theta and Sigma Nu, those were the only fraternities we had.

speaker: SS
words: What were the advantages to a student to being in a fraternity then?

speaker: LB
words: Just the same as it is now. No different. Friendship and a place to

speaker: LB
words: live and so forth and so on. Just exactly the same as now.

speaker: SS
words: I understand that the freshmen were in a somewhat disadvantaged po

speaker: SS
words: sition in regard to the older students.

speaker: LB
words: Oh, surely. They were razzed. They've given up that now, mostly.

speaker: SS
words: Had to wear green hats?

speaker: LB
words: All freshmen on campus had to wear green hats. If you didn't wear a

speaker: LB
words: green hat on campus, you got paddled by the upper classmen. And I

speaker: LB
words: mean paddled; if an upper classman, junior or senior, caught a fresh

speaker: LB
words: man without- and we were all known, everybody knew who the freshman

speaker: LB
words: were, if they caught you without a green beanie, why, someone would

speaker: LB
words: rush and get a paddle and bend you over their knees and paddle you!

speaker: LB
words: (Chuckles) They had traditions. Of course, that's what made the

speaker: LB
words: institution; juniors and seniors could wear corduroy trousers. Every

speaker: LB
words: junior and senior wore^corduroy pants. And as soon as you got to be

speaker: LB
words: a junior, the first thing you did was to pay six dollars for a pair

speaker: LB
words: of corduroy pants. And the rule was; you never washed 'em! The dirtier they got, the

speaker: LB
words: better! (Laughter) Just what they do now with their

speaker: SS
words: That was fashicn?

speaker: LB
words: That was fashion. You didn't wash them. If they stood up by them

speaker: LB
words: selves, that was fine! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: I'm surprised Dean French tolerated that. But then, I guess was the

speaker: SS
words: dean of women.

speaker: LB
words: She was the dean of women. And of course, the seniors carried canes.

speaker: LB
words: Senior men carried canes.

speaker: SS
words: Did they?

speaker: LB
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: I didn't think they would be that old by then!

speaker: LB
words: Well, just as a distinction. You knew he was a senior because he car

speaker: LB
words: ried a cane. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: What about the senior sneak? Was that a real institution?

speaker: LB
words: Yeah. Oh, they passed the word around, or else a meeting. I think

speaker: LB
words: it was the senior class meeting, and they'd decide on a certain day.

speaker: LB
words: And of course, the faculty was always advised of it. They weren't sup

speaker: LB
words: posed to be, but they always knew, and so that day the seniors would

speaker: LB
words: sneak out of the house early and have rented two or three vans or a

speaker: LB
words: stage and go to Coeur d'Alene or Lewiston or Elk River or something

speaker: LB
words: for a picnic and get home about nine o'clock at night. It was the

speaker: LB
words: senior sneak. And you were not docked for having missed class. It

speaker: LB
words: was just one of the things that was done; the senior sneak. I guess

speaker: LB
words: the sororities do it to this day. Maybe the sororities and fraterni

speaker: LB
words: ties do it up to now, I don't know, but they did for a good many years

speaker: LB
words: later, but not the whole class.

speaker: SS
words: What about the Hulme fight? Or how do you say that?

speaker: LB
words: Hume. Well, it was named for Dean Hulme, who was a very,

speaker: LB
words: very popular teacher. I never took a course under him because I was

speaker: LB
words: in engineering and didn't have time, but he was a very popular teacher.

speaker: LB
words: And he had a world reputation. And he was a good joe, and he encouraged

speaker: LB
words: them to have a Hulme fight. It was a case of getting a long rope and

speaker: LB
words: stringing it across Paradise Creek and the freshmen on one side

speaker: LB
words: and the sophomores on the other and see who'd get dunked. And then,

speaker: LB
words: during the evening, why, the freshmen would roam the town looking for

speaker: LB
words: sophomores to paddle them and the sophomores would look for the fresh

speaker: LB
words: men to paddle them and they formed an army and they'd march around

speaker: LB
words: Main Street and up and down Third. And the sophomores were always in

speaker: LB
words: the minority, so they usually kept out of the way, although both sides

speaker: LB
words: claimed victory. That was that. Something to do. There wasn't any

speaker: LB
words: thing else to do.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds like institutionalizing what otherwise could be more rowdy.

speaker: LB
words: Yes, uh-huh. That's right.

speaker: SS
words: And disorganized. Well, what about the rivalry with Washington State?

speaker: LB
words: Very severe. More so than now. When Washington State would play Idaho

speaker: LB
words: in football, they would wear their rooters caps. The WSU cap.gray and

speaker: LB
words: red with a WSU across it. The object was for Idaho students to swipe

speaker: LB
words: them. And when Idaho went to Pullman we went by train; hired a special

speaker: LB
words: train, three or four cars and they'd all go over to WSU by train and

speaker: LB
words: march from the train to the campus for the football game. March as a

speaker: LB
words: body. And we all wore our I caps. And the object was for the WSU

speaker: LB
words: students to swipe an I cap. And so, if some man, possibly some women,

speaker: LB
words: would stray five feet away from the line of march, why, some WSU Stu

speaker: LB
words: dent would dart out and grab the cap and he'd be protected by his fel

speaker: LB
words: low students and he got away. I had a WSU cap for years, I don't know

speaker: LB
words: what happened to it. (Chuckles) But got one some place.

speaker: LB
words: BOAS 33

speaker: SS
words: But wasn't Idaho badly outmatched in the football games?

speaker: LB
words: Not as badly then as we are now. Not as badly then. No.

speaker: SS
words: But what about the bonfire? Was that a regular ?

speaker: LB
words: That was a regular event the night before the WSU game. They had the

speaker: LB
words: bonfire on what is now Ghormley Field. At that time, I called it re

speaker: LB
words: creation park, but it was just an open field, there was no baseball

speaker: LB
words: diamond, there was nothing on it, it was just a piece of ground. And

speaker: LB
words: for a week they would gather used lumber, used boxes, cardboard boxes,

speaker: LB
words: wood, anything they could possibly get to build a huge bonefire, about

speaker: LB
words: the size of this house. And they always had to be topped with a privy!

speaker: LB
words: So then, about eight o'clock at night, when it got dark, they'd light

speaker: LB
words: the bonfire and have a rally. And the cheer leaders would lead the

speaker: LB
words: students in rallies; singing the Idaho fight song and cheering the

speaker: LB
words: team. And after forty minutes it would break up and the fire would

speaker: LB
words: die down somebody would watch it through the night so that it

speaker: LB
words: wouldn't spread. And that was that! Just a bonfire! They had a lot

speaker: LB
words: of those things. They had to do something to amuse besides now,

speaker: LB
words: they go to the beer halls.

speaker: SS
words: One other one that struck me, was the pajama parade.

speaker: LB
words: Well, the pajama parade was I think was-

speaker: SS
words: Was through downtown. And it looked to me as though it was kind of

speaker: SS
words: a human chain.

speaker: LB
words: Yes. Seems to me it was only girls, but I could be wrong. Maybe it

speaker: LB
words: was boys and girls. Wasn't downtown so much, as it was through the

speaker: LB
words: dormitories and halls. And they would be lead by the cheer leaders,

speaker: LB
words: and they all wore pajamas and stocking caps, and they would parade

speaker: LB
words: through the house at dinnertime or right after dinner, yelling and

speaker: LB
words: shouting and swiping souvenirs on the way and so forth and so on. Just

speaker: LB
words: a matter of letting off steam but cheering the team. In other words,

speaker: LB
words: it was an expression of student unity and student support for the ball

speaker: LB
words: team or the basket ball team. I don't recall whether the pajama parade

speaker: LB
words: was before a basketball game or a football game. I think it was foot

speaker: LB
words: ball.

speaker: SS
words: How strong would you say, in looking back at it, was the community

speaker: SS
words: spirit?

speaker: LB
words: Much stronger. Much stronger.

speaker: SS
words: Would you call it a real community?

speaker: LB
words: Ves. Uh-huh. The stores always closed for the Idaho-WSU game. A

speaker: LB
words: store would never stay open. I don't know if they stay open now or

speaker: LB
words: not. Some of 'em may close, but it was just universal. They just

speaker: LB
words: closed. That was that. Grocery stores and everything.

speaker: SS
words: But on the campus, itself, with the small size of the student body

speaker: SS
words: as it was, did that make a very closely knit relationship?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, I think it did. Decidedly. For basketball games, I was writing

speaker: LB
words: for half a dozen newspapers my junior and senior year ; I had a string

speaker: LB
words: of papers and we had to file 'em by telegraph. The Associated Press

speaker: LB
words: was a lot slower than it is now, because it was operated by the Morse

speaker: LB
words: code. And that's true; the Spokesman Review, the^main wire was taken

speaker: LB
words: down in Morse code by an operator. I worked on the Idaho Statesman

speaker: LB
words: when I quit the Westinghouse Electric Company for a year. And they

speaker: LB
words: received their Associated Press news by Morse code. I don't know how

speaker: LB
words: fast a man could operate, maybe twenty-five words a minute. Well when

speaker: LB
words: they got teletypes, it was sixty words a minute and now I don't know

speaker: LB
words: how fast it is, but it's a lot faster. They get more news. Well,

speaker: LB
words: and the papers that served Idaho.attempted to cover the news, they would

speaker: LB
words: have correspondents. I wrote for the Salt Lake Tribune, the Portland

speaker: LB
words: Oregonian, the Seattle Post-Intelligencer and the Seattle Times and

speaker: LB
words: the Spokesman Review. And I would try to write two stories so that

speaker: LB
words: they wouldn't see each other. And my wife and I attended the basket

speaker: LB
words: ball game and we'd run to the telegraph office to get ahead of the one

speaker: LB
words: or two other correspondents that wrote for other newspapers. Joel

speaker: LB
words: Priest, a good friend of mine, had the Idaho Statesman and I guess he

speaker: LB
words: had the other paper in Salt Lake. But the first that got there was

speaker: LB
words: the first one to have the privilege of getting it on the wire. We

speaker: LB
words: used to run down, and of course, the basketball games were then played

speaker: LB
words: in what is now the women's gym. It only held five or six hundred peo

speaker: LB
words: ple. We used to stand in line for an hour to get in. When we played

speaker: LB
words: the Pacific Coast Conference games here, and I wrote that up for the

speaker: LB
words: brochure at the dedication of Kibbe Dome. I don't know if you saw that

speaker: LB
words: little brochure.

speaker: SS
words: No, I haven't seen that yet.

speaker: LB
words: Haven't you seen it?

speaker: SS
words: No, I knew about it.

speaker: LB
words: Well, I wrote a story in there on the support of athletics in the

speaker: LB
words: early days. And we won the Pacific Coast Conference and we were in

speaker: LB
words: the Coast Conference. We defeated California in best two out of three

speaker: LB
words: games. And it was held here, and of^the Californians from Berekeley

speaker: LB
words: thought the gym was a crackerbox, but it was a standard sized basket

speaker: LB
words: ball court, but the seats were right up to the line. I think we went

speaker: LB
words: out to take a sandwich with us, but we went to stand in line about five

speaker: LB
words: o'clock in the afternoon to get in. And the game didn't start until

speaker: LB
words: eight o'clock or seven-thirty and we had to stand there for two hours

speaker: LB
words: to get in. And of course, we won the Conference championship. And

speaker: LB
words: there was the Northern Division and the Southern .division. We defeated Washington

speaker: LB
words: for the Northern Division and California defeated-

speaker: LB
words: I don't know who, for the Southern Division and the championship was

speaker: LB
words: decided here. Next year, it would go to the Southern Division, but we

speaker: LB
words: had it here and we won the title two years. Coast championship for the

speaker: LB
words: Pacific Coast Conference. One year on strictly percentage basis and

speaker: LB
words: the other year by meeting the divisional leaders.

speaker: SS
words: Was Upham the president at that time?

speaker: LB
words: I believe so. Lindley was the president when I first came here. Can't

speaker: LB
words: think of his name now. He went from here to Kansas. Ernest K. Lindsey,

speaker: LB
words: He had a son here who was Ernest K. Lindsey, Jr., and he played basket

speaker: LB
words: ball. He was a big fellow about six foot-three. And he later became

speaker: LB
words: editor of News Week magazine. And he was the biographer of Franklin

speaker: LB
words: D. Roosevelt. And he had been out here a couple of times to renew acquaintances.

speaker: LB
words: In April they had the fiftieth anniversary of it. It

speaker: LB
words: was publicized. Did you see it?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yes. Of the airmail flight.

speaker: LB
words: Well, that was the first flight. Yeah. That was the first sisiaail

speaker: LB
words: flight in the nation. And it was Varney Airlines. And they were to

speaker: LB
words: start from Pasco going east to Salt Lake and then transfer from Salt

speaker: LB
words: Lake to faster train going East and then start from Salt Lake West

speaker: LB
words: going to Pasco and from Pasco to be distributed to Seattle and Portland

speaker: LB
words: by train. And Boise made quite a celebration of it. I was on the

speaker: LB
words: city desk of the Idaho Statesman, and they had a big parade in the

speaker: LB
words: morning; I'm guessing at ten o'clock. And of course, in those days

speaker: LB
words: on every occasion they had a parade; that was the thing to do. And

speaker: LB
words: they scheduled an outdoor dance on the plaza in front of the State

speaker: LB
words: Capitol for that evening. Well, the airmail plane came in from Pasco

speaker: LB
words: at oh, eleven, eleven-fifteen, maybe eleven-thirty in the morning, and

speaker: LB
words: the pilot was Leon Cuddyback, who later became the administrator

speaker: LB
words: for the FAA at Seattle. He's still living; he's past eighty, but

speaker: LB
words: he's still living. And he was in Boise a few weeks ago for the cele

speaker: LB
words: bration. And the mayor was there at the airfield, which is where

speaker: LB
words: Boise state University now is and it was a small biplane and the

speaker: LB
words: mayor was there and I believe Senator Borah was there. And there

speaker: LB
words: was a small package of mail for Boise and a larger one for Salt Lake.

speaker: LB
words: They had an afternoon paper there called the Capitol News. Well, my

speaker: LB
words: photographer and I were on top of the Owyhee Hotel and he took some

speaker: LB
words: pictures of the parade and then we went down to the field and he got

speaker: LB
words: a picture of the first airmail pouch being handed to the mayor and

speaker: LB
words: so forth. And the Capitol News went to press around two-thirty as

speaker: LB
words: an afternoon paper and of course they had the break on the story and

speaker: LB
words: so they wrote the story up and they had pictures of course of the event

speaker: LB
words: and they told how the first mail came in on time and the package was

speaker: LB
words: given to the mayor and the northbound flight came in at two o'clock

speaker: LB
words: from Salt Lake and on to Pasco and so forth. And along about four

speaker: LB
words: o'clock, why, we began to hear rumors that the plane coming north had

speaker: LB
words: not arrived. And the managing editor was Horatio Miller and he wrote

speaker: LB
words: under the byline of '- the Censor, and he was managing editor and

speaker: LB
words: so he began to get interested and about a quarter to five he became

speaker: LB
words: more interested and we could see off in the southwest over the Owyhee

speaker: LB
words: Mountains, which were about ninety miles south of Boise,* ** dark, black

speaker: LB
words: clouds. So he put a call in to Salt Lake and found out that the plane

speaker: LB
words: had lef t.-j^ohe began to get a little nervous, and he called up say,

speaker: LB
words: Twin Falls or Rupert- and whether he heard that the plane had been seen

speaker: LB
words: going over^I do not remember, but along about six o'clock or six-thirty

speaker: LB
words: why then it was apparent that the plane was down. And the telephone

speaker: LB
words: office, the Intermountain Telephone Company was right next door to the

speaker: LB
words: Statesman. So Miller went over about six-thirty or seven o'clock and

speaker: LB
words: saw the chief operator and asked her to put one of the long distance

speaker: LB
words: operators at our disposal. He gave her instructions to contact through

speaker: LB
words: other exchanges every farmer in Southwest Idaho who had a telephone.

speaker: LB
words: And so he'd get on one phone and I was on the other and some of 'em

speaker: LB
words: had seen the plane and most of 'em had not. And so, in those daysyou

speaker: LB
words: used earphones on radios, it was the beginning of radio in 1925,

speaker: LB
words: and radio was an oddity and very few people had them. And so he deci

speaker: LB
words: ded, I didn't, he decided to put out a special edition and get it out

speaker: LB
words: about ten o'clock and go up to the big dance at the Capitol and sell

speaker: LB
words: 'em and sell 'em on the streets. So we put out a special edition and

speaker: LB
words: that means just change the front page enough to get one story on it,

speaker: LB
words: and the rest of it was just the same as yesterday's paper. And we

speaker: LB
words: put out a special and I learned a very valuable lesson, that you never

speaker: LB
words: anticipate*news. Well, they didn't hear from the man for five days.

speaker: LB
words: And he landed over in Jordan Valley and he wrecked the plane and it

speaker: LB
words: took him five days to walk out to a farmhouse where they had a telephone,

speaker: LB
words: I guess he went to a couple before that, but he finally walked out to

speaker: LB
words: where they had a telephone. His name was Rose. Don't know his first

speaker: LB
words: name*

speaker: SS
words: And this was the return flight.

speaker: LB
words: The return flight. The first flight was south to Salt Lake and the

speaker: LB
words: return flight was Salt Lake to Pasco. And it was return flight.

speaker: SS
words: Had the other newspaper- was there another newspaper in Boise?

speaker: LB
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: SS
words: They had already gone out with the story that-

speaker: LB
words: It was an afternoon paper and they included in their story the fact

speaker: LB
words: that the morning plane came in and there was a big celebration and

speaker: LB
words: so forth. That the afternoon plane came in approximately on time

speaker: LB
words: and deposited it's package and had gone on to Pasco. Of course, there

speaker: LB
words: was no celebration for the afternoon , but they just included

speaker: LB
words: in their story that the flight had gone on to Pasco.

speaker: SS
words: It must have caused consternation at the dance.

speaker: LB
words: Oh, yes, it did. Well, yeah. (Chuckles) But it made the other paper

speaker: LB
words: look silly because they anticipated the news, you see. But he had run

speaker: LB
words: into the thunder and lightning storm over the Owyhee Mountains and he

speaker: LB
words: had tried to skirt it and got lost. And of course, at that time, all

speaker: LB
words: they had was an ordinary compass and he got lost and didn't know where

speaker: LB
words: he was and put down in the field down in Jordan Valley. If you've

speaker: LB
words: ever been Jordan Valley you'd know that you could walk for five

speaker: LB
words: days and not find anything. So he had walked out. I don't know, but

speaker: LB
words: he walked in circles, and he finally walked out and got to a telephone.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get a lot of good newspaper experience working on the States

speaker: SS
words: man?

speaker: LB
words: Oh, yes. Yes. It was a good paper then. I don't see it now anymore.

speaker: LB
words: Of course, newspapering in those days was much different than it is

speaker: LB
words: now. When I ran the Idahonian, if you've looked at the issues for

speaker: LB
words: the ten years before I quit; I had a rule of twenty-five stories on

speaker: LB
words: page one. Now, they run four stories on page one. The Idaho Statesman

speaker: LB
words: when I worked there would run forty to fifty stories on page one. And

speaker: LB
words: I still think that is preferable. I have yet to find anyone among my

speaker: LB
words: friends who read these long, indepth stories. There was one last night.

speaker: LB
words: I can't read all of 'em. I read that much and I'm all through, and

speaker: LB
words: they run them for two columns. I don't think one person in fifty reads

speaker: LB
words: the whole thing except those about whom it's written or their friends.

speaker: LB
words: I prefer to see them ten-inch stories and run a lot of 'em. Give 'em

speaker: LB
words: the news. I get more damn news out of the Wall Street Journal than I

speaker: LB
words: do out of any newspaper, because they've got one column, right there,

speaker: LB
words: and they carry more news of the world in that much than you get out

speaker: LB
words: of any daily paper that I've seen. And I tried to get twenty-five

speaker: LB
words: on-page one, I didn't always make it. Sometimes there'd be eighteen,

speaker: LB
words: sometimes twenty-one. But I would run a half a column story from the

speaker: LB
words: top of the page to the fold; that was about enough.

speaker: SS
words: What was your job in Boise on the paper?

speaker: LB
words: City editor.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a demanding job? Did you try to cover the city?

speaker: LB
words: No, no. At that time the Idaho Statesman had seven reporters. One

speaker: LB
words: of 'em Glenn Balch, who is still living, and he writes children's

speaker: LB
words: stories and he has for the last forty years. He was riding circuit.

speaker: LB
words: He'd spend one day in Glenns Ferry and one day in Rupert and one day

speaker: LB
words: in Twin Falls and one day in Gooding and then about ten o'clock at

speaker: LB
words: night he would phone in his news. And once every month he'd drop into

speaker: LB
words: Boise for a Sunday. He was single. And we had one man rode circuit

speaker: LB
words: from Boise to Caldwell and Nampa and back on the electric Interurban

speaker: LB
words: Line. He'd do that every day. Leave about nine o'clock in the mor

speaker: LB
words: ning and stop off at Caldwell and get the local news of Caldwell at

speaker: LB
words: the courthouse and so forth and then go to Nampa. The trains ran

speaker: LB
words: about once an hour. Just one car; called it the Interurban.

speaker: SS
words: So they would get the news at the courthouse?

speaker: LB
words: At the courthouse or he'd go to the Chamber of Commerce or anyplace

speaker: LB
words: else. And once in a while he'd stop at Meridian and once in a while

speaker: LB
words: he'd stop at Star, get local news. Now of course, they do it by

speaker: LB
words: having a paid correspondent. But as the city editor I made assignments to the other

speaker: LB
words: three reporters or four, if there was something

speaker: LB
words: special and the copy came to me and I would edit it and write the head

speaker: LB
words: lines on it and pass it over to the managing editor, and if he liked

speaker: LB
words: it he would take it as is and if he didn't like it, he would either

speaker: LB
words: cut it himself or say, "I don't like the headline, Louie." Or so forth

speaker: LB
words: and so on. He was managing editor. He ran it. And the opposite to

speaker: LB
words: me was the telegraph editor; had the telegraph news. He would write

speaker: LB
words: that; world news. And the managing editor would find out from the

speaker: LB
words: business manager or the composing room the number of inches we had

speaker: LB
words: available for news. So at seven o'clock at night he would say we ' "

speaker: LB
words: a hole of so many inches. And the city would have forty percent of

speaker: LB
words: that hole and the telegraph editor would have sixty percent, or visa

speaker: LB
words: versa. And you had a sheet and every story that came in I was allowed

speaker: LB
words: say fifteen hundred inches and IA every story and estimate that it

speaker: LB
words: would be five inches or seven inches or so forth, and keep a running

speaker: LB
words: total of numbers of words- or numbers of inches- and just the beginning

speaker: LB
words: of the headlines so I'd know what it is and I could look back and see

speaker: LB
words: what the story was. And when you got full, you quit. That was the job.

speaker: LB
words: I don't know how they do it now. They probably say-

speaker: SS
words: Before we get into the newspaper business, we should save that for

speaker: SS
words: next time.

speaker: SS
words: END OF FIRST INTERVIEW
